entries.summary: OpenStreetMap completed its migration from CC-BY-SA 2.0 to ODbL on 12 September 2012, making ODbL the share-alike copyleft of choice for large-scale geospatial data. The OSM community chose ODbL for two reasons the old Creative Commons license could not deliver: first, ODbL targets databases and the EU sui-generis database right rather than copyright over creative works, giving OSM firmer legal footing for a compiled dataset of factual traces; second, ODbL's Collective Database versus Derivative Database distinction lets third parties combine an unmodified OSM extract with independently licensed data (as a Collective Database) without triggering share-alike on the other components, which is essential for commercial map stacks that must mix OSM with proprietary sources. About 1% of contributor data was lost during the relicensing because some contributors declined the new terms; the community has since rebuilt most of the gap. ODbL is now also used by OpenCorporates, Open Food Facts, Paris OpenData, and Overture Maps.

sources.excerpt: OpenStreetMap data is made available for reuse under the Open Database License (ODbL) share-alike license for data. Before 12 September 2012 OpenStreetMap was using the CC-BY-SA 2.0 license. The change to ODbL was made for multiple reasons and followed a lengthy process during which the license was developed. Data published before 12 September is still available under the old CC-BY-SA license. ... The ODbL was developed by the Open Knowledge Foundation. Most contributors agree to the new terms, however some did not and some 1% of data was lost in the conversion.

OpenStreetMap (OSM) project completed the move from a Creative Commons license to ODbL in September 2012 in an attempt to have more legal security and a more specific license for databases rather than creative works. Other projects using ODbL include OpenCorporates, Open Data Blend, Open Food Facts, Paris OpenData, and Overture Maps.

entries.summary: ODbL draws a sharp line between Collective Databases (aggregating an unmodified ODbL database next to independent databases) and Derivative Databases (databases that adapt, rearrange, or extract a Substantial part of the Contents). Only Derivative Databases trigger the Share-Alike obligation in §4.4. §4.5(a) expressly states that you are not required to license the Collective Database under ODbL, though ODbL still governs the embedded component. This structure is what makes ODbL commercially workable: a publisher can ship an OSM layer in a bundle alongside proprietary mapping data without relicensing the bundle, as long as the OSM layer itself is unmodified and separately available under ODbL. Extraction or Re-utilisation of a Substantial part of the Contents into a new database is, by §4.4(b), a Derivative Database that must comply with Share-Alike.

sources.source: Open Data Commons — ODbL v1.0 (§4.4 and §4.5 text)
sources.summary: Canonical Open Data Commons publication of ODbL v1.0 including the §1 definitions of Collective Database and Derivative Database and the §4.4 Share-Alike / §4.5 Limits of Share-Alike text that operationalise the distinction.

entries.summary: ODbL §4.6 is the data-license analog of the GPL's source-disclosure requirement. When a licensee publicly uses a Derivative Database (or a Produced Work derived from one), the licensee must offer recipients a machine-readable copy of either (a) the entire Derivative Database or (b) a file containing all the alterations made to the original Database, such that the differences between the original and the derivative can be reconstructed. The Derivative Database or alteration file must be provided at no more than reasonable production cost for physical distribution and free of charge over the internet. This is what gives ODbL its copyleft teeth for data: share-alike in §4.4 binds the license of downstream databases, and §4.6 ensures those downstream databases are actually available to the community.

sources.source: Open Data Commons — ODbL v1.0 (§4.6 Access to Derivative Databases)
sources.summary: Canonical Open Data Commons publication of ODbL v1.0 containing the §4.6 Access to Derivative Databases clause: the publicly-used Derivative Database or its alteration file must be offered in machine-readable form at no more than reasonable production cost for physical distributions and free over the internet.
